stem: A hospital uses a tTG-IgA antibody test to screen for celiac disease. The test has a sensitivity of 80% and a specificity of 81%. The prevalence of celiac disease in the patient population is estimated to be between 9.7% and 20.0%, with uncertainty due to regional variation and demographic differences in the hospital's catchment area. A patient receives a positive test result. What is the probability that the patient actually has celiac disease? Provide your answer as a probability with a confidence interval reflecting the uncertainty in prevalence.

ground_truth.answer: P(celiac disease | positive test) ranges from 0.311 to 0.513 as prevalence varies from 9.7% to 20.0%. Point estimate at 14.9% prevalence: 0.424.

solution_steps: Apply Bayes' theorem: P(D|+) = P(+|D)×P(D) / [P(+|D)×P(D) + P(+|¬D)×P(¬D)], where P(+|¬D) = 1-specificity = 0.19.
The confidence interval [0.311, 0.513] reflects the range of posterior probabilities over plausible prevalence values.
common_failure_modes: Confusing sensitivity (80%) with positive predictive value
Providing only a point estimate without acknowledging the prevalence uncertainty
Ignoring the base rate (prevalence) and using the test sensitivity as the answer
Not recognizing the strong base rate effect when prevalence is low
